network.overall_strategy: The synthesis network for threonine integrates five literature-validated routes: cyanosulfidic Strecker via lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde (Surface UV photochemistry), Miller-Urey spark discharge (Surface electrical simulation), speculative aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde (Biochemical with mineral aid), reductive amination of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate (Hydrothermal Fe-catalyzed), and Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway (Surface). Hubs at acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and glyceraldehyde enable convergence. Hydrothermal provides reducing precursors like acetaldehyde; Surface drives photochemistry and condensation; Biochemical assembles via low-temp routes to address threonine's instability. 10 pathways trace diverse routes with environmental flow, emphasizing low-temp Surface/Biochemical for stability.

reactions.name: Photoredox formation of acetaldehyde from HCN
reactions.mechanism: UV photoredox cycling with H2S as reductant converts HCN to acetaldehyde via formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde intermediates.
reactions.reasoning: UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S produces acetaldehyde at 19% yield for related steps (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013); TiO2 aids photocatalysis (Dunn et al., 1981).

reactions.name: Spark discharge synthesis of glycine and acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Spark discharge generates HCN and aldehydes in situ, followed by Strecker to glycine; acetaldehyde produced directly.
reactions.reasoning: Spark discharge on CH4/NH3/H2O produces threonine precursors including glycine and acetaldehyde (Ring et al., 1972; Parker et al., 2011); montmorillonite concentrates products.

reactions.name: Photoreduction to lactaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde + HCN forms cyanohydrin, photoreduced to lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde cyanohydrin photoreduction yields lactaldehyde at 19% (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013); part of cyanosulfidic pathway (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis via lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde + NH3 forms imine, + HCN adds to aminonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Classic Strecker on lactaldehyde yields threonine aminonitrile (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015); montmorillonite catalyzes (Kitadai et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminonitrile to threonine
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis of nitrile to carboxylic acid, yielding racemic threonine/allothreonine.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis completes Strecker to threonine (Patel et al., 2015); low temp addresses instability (Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Strecker via glyceraldehyde to threonine aminonitrile
reactions.reasoning: Parallel Strecker from glyceraldehyde in cyanosulfidic network (Patel et al., 2015); borate stabilizes (Powner et al., 2009).

reactions.name: Speculative aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Reverse aldol: glycine + acetaldehyde condense to threonine, thermodynamically unfavorable but aided by concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Conceptual reverse of threonine aldolase; unfavorable but possible with high glycine/acetaldehyde (Fesko, 2016); montmorillonite aids condensation (Bujdák et al., 1995); speculative prebiotic extension.

reactions.name: Fe-catalyzed formation of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate
reactions.mechanism: CO-dependent chain elongation from acetaldehyde and cyano ligands on Ni-Fe surfaces to keto acid.
reactions.reasoning: FeS catalyzes α-keto acid formation analogous to pyruvate (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 2006; Cody et al., 2000); greigite/mackinawite in vents (White et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of α-keto-β-hydroxybutyrate
reactions.mechanism: Fe2+ promotes reductive amination with NH3 to amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Fe2+ catalyzes reductive amination of α-keto acids at 70°C (Muchowska et al., 2019); extended to threonine analog with H2/Ni but Fe primary (Kaur et al., 2024); pyrite for photocatalysis if needed (2024 Nature Comm.).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs via lactaldehyde to hydantoin then threonine
reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde + HCN + NH3 + CO2 to hydantoin intermediate, hydrolysis to threonine.
reactions.reasoning: Bucherer-Bergs for amino acids via hydantoin sink (Pulletikurti et al., 2022); hydantoins in meteorites (Lerner and Cooper, 2005); clay aids.

reactions.name: Formose-like to glyceraldehyde from glycolaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Aldol homologation in cyanosulfidic context to glyceraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde in nucleotide pathways (Patel et al., 2015); borate stabilizes sugars (Ricardo et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reduction to glycolaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction of HCN/H2S on magnetite to glycolaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal FT synthesis produces aldehydes (McCollom et al., 1999); magnetite catalyzes (Huber and Wächtershäuser, 1998); feeds surface.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Strecker via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Surface UV from HCN to acetaldehyde to lactaldehyde to threonine.

pathways.name: Spark discharge Strecker
pathways.description: Surface spark produces glycine/acetaldehyde, then Strecker via lactaldehyde.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic via glyceraldehyde
pathways.description: Surface HCN to glycolaldehyde (hydrothermal feed) to glyceraldehyde to threonine.

pathways.name: Speculative aldol from glycine and acetaldehyde
Hydrothermal reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal to keto acid, then amination; acetaldehyde from surface feedback.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Surface hydantoin route from lactaldehyde hub.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal feed to surface Strecker via lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Hydrothermal glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde to surface lactaldehyde path.

pathways.name: Spark to glyceraldehyde Strecker
pathways.description: Spark acetaldehyde to glycolaldehyde homologation to glyceraldehyde path.

pathways.name: Integrated hydrothermal-surface reductive to Strecker
pathways.description: Hydrothermal keto acid alternative, but converge to aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Full cyanosulfidic with spark boost
pathways.description: Spark enhances NH3 for glyceraldehyde/lactaldehyde dual convergence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong alignment with cyanosulfidic Strecker via lactaldehyde; well-supported by literature (Ritson & Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). Minor issue with environmental transition to biochemical hydrolysis.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalysts for surface photoredox.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Spark discharge produces glycine and aldehydes, but direct link to threonine Strecker via acetaldehyde is indirect; aligns with Miller-Urey (Ring et al., 1972), but rxn_002 notation imprecise.
suggestions: Clarify spark inputs to include HCN/H2S for better threonine yield as per Parker et al., 2011.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strecker via glyceraldehyde supported as parallel route (Patel et al., 2015); formose-like step plausible but HCN role unclear.
suggestions: Refine rxn_011 to pure aldol without HCN for better formose alignment.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Aldol condensation speculative and thermodynamically unfavorable (Fesko, 2016); no prebiotic demo, though conceptually simple.
suggestions: Add mineral catalyst like montmorillonite for feasibility (Kitadai et al., 2015).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reductive amination aligns with Fe-catalyzed routes (Muchowska et al., 2019), but keto acid formation from acetaldehyde+HCN lacks direct support.
suggestions: Justify rxn_008 with literature on keto acid synthesis, e.g., via CO-dependent elongation (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2006).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bucherer-Bergs well-supported for amino acids (Pulletikurti et al., 2022); hydantoin intermediate plausible for threonine.
suggestions: Include CO2 source for ammonium carbonate in rxn_010.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent hydrothermal-to-surface flow; aligns with environmental transitions and greigite catalysis in vents (White et al., 2015).
suggestions: Specify FeS minerals for rxn_012 reduction.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Spark to glyceraldehyde Strecker; spark promotes hydroxylated AAs (Parker et al., 2011), but rxn_002 imprecise.
suggestions: Integrate H2S in spark for better threonine yield.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Integrated hydrothermal-surface; reductive to Strecker, but short steps limit detail; addresses instability via low-temp biochemical.
suggestions: Elaborate connection between reductive amination product and Strecker.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full cyanosulfidic with spark boost; comprehensive but lengthy; aligns with protometabolism (Patel et al., 2015).
suggestions: Streamline to avoid redundant spark step.

evaluation.network_feedback: Well-connected network with strong literature alignment to five key routes; addresses threonine instability via low-temp environments. Minor issues in speculative reactions (e.g., aldol, keto acid formation) and imprecise spark notation reduce feasibility slightly, but topology and convergence are excellent.
